articles.title: If At First You Fail Miserbly At The Supreme Court, Try, Try Again — See Generally
articles.content:
重复同样的事情两次却期待不同结果就是疯狂：特朗普想要最高法院重新审理刚刚驳回其出生公民权行政令的案件，并要求“立即”再审。过程中他还顺带侮辱了法院。

申诉研究：一个保守派监督组织对罗伯塔·卡普兰提出了律师执业投诉，而特朗普仍在试图逃避欠E·让·卡罗尔的近580万美元。

红线：苏利文·克伦威尔曾向合伙人承诺不介入特朗普的卡罗尔案件，结果还是介入了，如今内部已经爆发内战。

庄家永远赢：一位国会议员要求约翰·罗伯茨解释，究竟是什么阻止了大法官和法官助理在Kalshi平台上对只有他们知道结果的案件下真金白银的赌注。

刑满释放：合并后的Cadwalader事务所系统互不兼容，遗留时间记录员需在7月20日前重新审计六个月内的六分钟计费条目。

未经审视的笔记本电脑不值得使用：芝加哥大学正在从一年级法学课堂上撤走笔记本电脑，作为对人工智能时代的回应，重新采用苏格拉底教学法。

难以企及：耶鲁大学在拒绝最多申请者的法学院排名中位居榜首，录取率最低的二十所法学院平均仅录取11%的申请者。

无国界的声誉：哈佛在夸克雷利·西蒙兹全球法学院排名中超越耶鲁和斯坦福，因为其他国家评判标准与美国新闻与世界报道的评分体系不同。

帖子《一次又一次在最高法院惨败后，再试一次——参见 generally》首次发表于Above the Law。
articles.summary:
Insanity Is Doing The Same Thing Twice And Expecting…: Trump wants the same Supreme Court that just rejected his birthright citizenship order to rehear the whole thing “immediately.” And he decided to insult them in the process.

Grievance Studies: A conservative watchdog group filed a bar complaint against Roberta Kaplan as Trump keeps trying to wriggle out of paying the nearly $5.8 million he owes E. Jean Carroll.

Redlined: Sullivan & Cromwell promised its partners it would stay out of Trump’s Carroll cases, then jumped in anyway, and now there’s a civil war.

The House Always Wins: A member of Congress wants John Roberts to explain what exactly stops justices and clerks from betting real money on Kalshi about cases that only they know the outcome of.

Time Served: Legacy Cadwalader timekeepers have until July 20 to re-audit six months of six-minute entries because the merged firm’s systems don’t talk to each other.

The Unexamined Laptop Is Not Worth Using: The University of Chicago is yanking laptops out of 1L classrooms and leaning back into the Socratic method as its answer to the AI era.

Hard To Get: Yale tops the list of law schools that reject the most applicants, with the twenty most selective schools admitting an average of just 11 percent.

Prestige Without Borders: Harvard edges out Yale and Stanford atop the Quacquarelli Symonds global law school rankings, because the rest of the world grades on a different curve than U.S. News.

The post If At First You Fail Miserbly At The Supreme Court, Try, Try Again — See Generally appeared first on Above the Law.

articles.title: ‘Adventures In Legal Tech’: You, Too, Can Tame Your ESI. Here’s How.
articles.content:
如果你的律所曾经将一堆文件移交用于证据开示，然后只能听天由命，你并不孤单。

来自ALSP Trustpoint.One的沃伦·帕里诺花了多年时间帮助独立执业者和小型律所管理其电子存储信息。

他使用的不是昂贵的软件或成群的审核人员，而是大多数律师要么不知道、要么直接跳过的几个基本方法。

在本期“法律科技探索”节目中，沃伦详细解析了一个工作流程，它可以在你为文档审核花一分钱之前就大幅削减审核成本。

他还讨论了附件方面发生了什么变化（剧透警告：老派的PDF现在已经是最不用担心的问题了），并准确解释AI什么时候该用、什么时候不该用。

AI会有帮助吗？这取决于情况

沃伦说，人工智能对于电子证据开示来说确实可能是一个“游戏规则改变者”。但它也可能是一个“账单膨胀器”。在这里，他分享了成功使用这项技术的先决条件。

2026年如何审查提供商

谁应该成为你的电子证据开示合作伙伴？在这里，沃伦讨论了合作伙伴与供应商之间的区别，以及2026年需要重点关注的特定技能。

收听完整对话

想了解更多吗？查看下面的节目。

这篇文章《“法律科技探索”：你也能驯服你的电子存储信息。方法如下。》首次发表于Above the Law。
articles.summary:
If your firm has ever handed over a pile of files for discovery and just hoped for the best, you’re not alone.

Warren Parrino of the ALSP Trustpoint.One has spent years helping solo practitioners and small firms get a handle on their electronically stored information. 

Not with expensive software or an army of reviewers, but with a few basics that most lawyers either don’t know about or skip right past.

In this episode of “Adventures in Legal Tech,” Warren unpacks the one workflow that can dramatically cut your review costs before you spend a dollar on document review. 

He also covers what’s changed with attachments (spoiler: the old-school PDF is the least of your worries now), and explains exactly when AI belongs in the picture, and when it doesn’t.

Will AI Help? It Depends

Artificial intelligence can certainly be a “game-changer” for eDiscovery, Warren says. But it can also be a “bill ballooner.” Here, he shares the prerequisites for successful use of this technology. 

Vetting Providers in 2026

Who should be your e-discovery partner? Here, Warren discusses the differences between a partner and a vendor, as well as the specific skills to focus on in 2026.

Hear the Full Conversations

Curious to learn more? Check out this episode below.

The post ‘Adventures In Legal Tech’: You, Too, Can Tame Your ESI. Here’s How.  appeared first on Above the Law.

articles.title: How Appealing Weekly Roundup
articles.content:
编者按：霍华德·巴什曼的"How Appealing"博客每周精选，该博客是首个专注于上诉诉讼的网络博客。更多故事请访问How Appealing。

“特朗普E·让·卡罗尔诽谤案工作引发苏利文·克伦威尔律师事务所内部分歧；该律所联席主席向合伙人保证不会介入总统针对性侵和诽谤案的上诉；在被特朗普私人律师要求后改变了立场”：华尔街日报的C·瑞安·巴伯、乔·帕拉佐洛和乔希·道西对此进行了报道。

“第七巡回法院裁定：伊利诺伊州AR-15步枪禁令符合宪法”：彭博法律网的梅根·克雷波对此进行了报道（完整访问需订阅）。

“保守派大法官在最新的总统权力裁决中附加了一些重大条件；最高法院不仅在将政治权力从国会转移到行政分支；还在为自己保留大量政治权力”：马迪巴·K·丹尼在Balls and Strikes网站发表了这篇评论文章。

“'Oyez, oyez.' 最高法院最后一位官方宣报员去世，享年102岁。15岁时，他开始在法院担任信使，后来晋升为宣报员，负责宣布大法官入场并敲槌宣布开庭。”华盛顿邮报的科尔·雷诺兹为此撰写了讣告。

“首席大法官小约翰·G·罗伯茨已将关于巡回法官瑞安·D·尼尔森的司法行为和残疾投诉第26-90153号移送至第四巡回法院司法委员会。”这是美国第九巡回上诉法院公共信息办公室今天发布的新闻稿的开头。

“最高法院本 term 回顾：扎克·谢姆托布；SCOTUSblog执行主编（也是大卫·拉特的丈夫）分享了2025年10月 term 的要点，包括最重要的裁决和主要主题。”您可以通过此链接访问大卫·拉特"Original Jurisdiction"播客的最新一集。

How Appealing每周精选一文最先发表于Above the Law。
articles.summary:
Ed. Note: A weekly roundup of just a few items from Howard Bashman’s How Appealing blog, the Web’s first blog devoted to appellate litigation. Check out these stories and more at How Appealing.

“Work on Trump’s E. Jean Carroll Defamation Case Divides Sullivan & Cromwell; Law firm’s co-chairman assured partners it would stay out of the president’s appeal of sex-abuse and defamation cases; It reversed course after being asked by Trump’s personal lawyer”: C. Ryan Barber, Joe Palazzolo, and Josh Dawsey of The Wall Street Journal have this report.

“Illinois Ban on AR-15s Is Constitutional, Seventh Circuit Rules”: Megan Crepeau of Bloomberg Law has this report (subscription required for full access).

“The Conservative Justices Attached Some Major Strings to the Latest Decision On Presidential Power; The Supreme Court is not only transferring political power from Congress to the executive branch; It is also reserving a ton of political power for itself”: Madiba K. Dennie has this essay online at Balls and Strikes.

“‘Oyez, oyez.’ Supreme Court’s last official crier is dead at 102. At age 15, he began working at the court as a page. He rose to become its crier, announcing the justices’ arrival and gaveling the court to order.” Cole Reynolds of The Washington Post has written this obituary.

“Chief Justice John G. Roberts, Jr. has transferred Judicial Conduct and Disability Complaint Number 26‑90153, regarding Circuit Judge Ryan D. Nelson, to the Judicial Council of the Fourth Circuit.” So begins a news release that the Public Information Office of the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit issued today.

“Supreme Court Term In Review: Zach Shemtob; The executive editor of SCOTUSblog (and [David Lat’s] husband) shares takeaways from October Term 2025, including the biggest rulings and major themes.” You can access today’s new episode of David Lat’s “Original Jurisdiction” podcast via this link.

The post How Appealing Weekly Roundup appeared first on Above the Law.

articles.title: Lawyers Do Not Need An AI Ban. They Need An AI Duty Of Care.
articles.content:
不久前，人工智能在法律行业的应用似乎还停留在假设阶段。律师们私下试探着使用它，法官们好奇却谨慎。大多数关于人工智能与法律的讨论听起来都像学术探讨。

那个阶段已经结束了。

如今，律师们正在使用人工智能起草诉状、汇总医疗记录、审查合同、整理证据材料、准备催款函以及进行研究工作。有些人在深思熟虑地使用，有些则在使用时鲁莽行事全国各地的法院开始察觉到这种差异。

而且，法官们越来越不耐烦了。

过去两年间，多家法院对提交包含人工智能系统生成虚假引用的诉状的律师进行了制裁。在一些案件中，律师坚称这些案例是真实存在的。在另一些案件中，他们承认在提交前从未核实过这些引用。这起初只是零星的尴尬事件，如今正在演变为更严重的问题：一个职业责任问题。

这很重要，因为法律行业从未仅靠诚信制度运作。律师受委托处理人们的财务、医疗记录、企业、家庭、自由，有时甚至是生命。这项职业之所以能够运转，全赖客户相信他们的律师在行使独立判断。

人工智能可以协助完成这些工作，但不能取代它。

新泽西州在这方面值得一些认可。与许多司法管辖区相比，该州的司法系统采取了更加积极主动的方式，认识到人工智能已成为现代法律实践的一部分。美国律师协会也发布了指导意见，提醒律师们现有职业道德义务已适用于人工智能的使用。专业能力仍然重要。保密性仍然重要。对法庭的坦诚仍然重要。

但仅有指导意见已经不够了。

问题不在于律师们正在使用人工智能，而在于许多律所仍然没有制定有意义的人工智能使用内部规则。

在一些机构中，年轻律师私下依赖人工智能工具而无人监督，因为他们认为每个人都在这样做。在另一些地方，律师将敏感的客户信息粘贴到公共系统中，却不完全理解这些信息的去向或日后可能如何被使用。有些律所根本没有书面政策。有些律所的政策含糊其辞，实际上不过是“小心使用”这样的话语。

这不是可持续的做法。

不久之后，法院和纪律机构将停止将这些事件视为成长中的阵痛，而开始将其视为职业管理的失败。

法律行业此前已经经历过类似的情况。电子邮件引发了保密问题。云存储提出了安全问题。远程工作改变了律所管理员工的方式。每一次技术变革都迫使律师们将道德标准适应现代现实。

人工智能不过是迄今为止最大规模的挑战。

部分问题在于人工智能制造了一种能力假象。文字通常听起来很专业。格式看起来很规范。答案瞬间呈现且充满自信。但自信不等于准确，快速不等于判断。

资深律师理解一些年轻律师仍在学习的东西：好的法律工作不仅仅是产出文字。而是知道哪些事实重要，识别缺失的内容，理解人类行为，预见风险，并行使自由裁量权。这些不是机械功能。

而是专业功能。

随着世界各国政府开始对法律行业之外的人工智能实施更广泛的监管，这一区别变得更加重要。欧盟已通过涵盖透明度、操纵和高风险自动化系统的全面人工智能规则。多个州正在考虑或实施涉及人工智能生成的互动和决策的披露要求和消费者保护措施。

方向是明确的。监管机构越来越相信人工智能系统可以以值得监督的方式影响人类行为。

律师们不应认为这个行业会以某种方式免于这场更大的对话。

律师们也不应犯下这样的错误：认为这个问题只涉及诉讼制裁或虚假引用。更大的危险可能更加隐蔽。

试想一位律师依赖人工智能汇总数千页医疗记录，却遗漏了图表中埋藏的关键标注。试想一家律所使用人工智能生成的接待系统，却无意中阻止了某些客户提出有效索赔。试想保密的和解讨论被输入到不安全的平台。试想客户发现他们被收取律师费，而实际工作主要由软件完成，从而引发计费纠纷。

这些情况即将到来。有些可能已经存在。

这个行业现在需要的不是恐慌，而是结构。

每家使用人工智能的律所都应有书面政策，涵盖保密、核实、监督、披露、计费实践和文件审查。律师应了解他们使用的是什么工具，这些工具会产生什么风险，以及存在什么保护客户的保障措施。律所应像培训信托账户处理或数据安全一样培训律师。

最重要的是，律师必须记住，道德责任不能委托给软件。

不能委托给ChatGPT。不能委托给Claude。不能委托给任何承诺效率的平台。

归根结底，提交文件上仍然写着律师的名字。

而这意味着律师仍对文件背后的判断负责。

迈克尔·J·爱泼斯坦是哈佛法学院毕业生，现为审判律师及爱泼斯坦律师事务所的Managing Partner，该公司总部位于新泽西州。

原文首次发表于Above the Law。
articles.summary:
Not long ago, artificial intelligence in the legal profession still felt hypothetical. Lawyers were experimenting with it quietly. Judges were curious but cautious. Most discussions about AI and the law sounded academic.

That phase is over.

Today, lawyers are using AI to draft motions, summarize medical records, review contracts, organize discovery, prepare demand letters, and conduct research. Some are using it thoughtfully. Some are using it recklessly. Courts across the country are beginning to see the difference.

And increasingly, judges are losing patience.

Over the last two years, courts have sanctioned attorneys for filing briefs containing fake citations generated by AI systems. In some cases, lawyers insisted the cases were real. In others, they admitted they never checked the citations before filing them. What began as isolated embarrassment is starting to become something more serious: a professional responsibility problem.

That matters because the legal profession has never operated on the honor system alone. Lawyers are entrusted with people’s finances, health records, businesses, families, liberty, and sometimes their lives. The profession works only if clients believe their lawyers are exercising independent judgment.

AI can assist with that work. It cannot replace it.

New Jersey deserves some credit here. Compared with many jurisdictions, the state’s judiciary has taken a more proactive approach in recognizing that AI is now part of modern legal practice. The American Bar Association has also issued guidance reminding attorneys that existing ethical duties already apply to AI use. Competence still matters. Confidentiality still matters. Candor to the court still matters.

But guidance alone is not enough anymore.

The problem is not that lawyers are using AI. The problem is that many firms still do not have meaningful internal rules governing how it should be used.

In some offices, younger associates are quietly relying on AI tools without supervision because they assume everyone else is doing the same. In others, lawyers are pasting sensitive client information into public systems without fully understanding where that information goes or how it may later be used. Some firms have no written policies at all. Others have policies so vague they amount to little more than “be careful.”

That is not sustainable.

At some point soon, courts and disciplinary authorities are going to stop treating these incidents as growing pains. They will begin treating them as failures of professional management.

The legal profession has already seen versions of this before. Email created confidentiality concerns. Cloud storage raised security questions. Remote work changed how firms supervised employees. Every technological shift forced lawyers to adapt ethical standards to modern reality.

AI is simply the largest version of that challenge so far.

Part of the issue is that AI creates an illusion of competence. The writing often sounds polished. The formatting looks professional. The answers arrive instantly and confidently. But confidence is not accuracy, and speed is not judgment.

Experienced lawyers understand something younger attorneys are still learning: good legal work is not just about producing words. It is about knowing which facts matter, recognizing what is missing, understanding human behavior, anticipating risk, and exercising discretion. Those are not mechanical functions.

They are professional ones.

That distinction becomes even more important as governments worldwide begin imposing broader AI regulations outside the legal profession itself. The European Union has adopted sweeping AI rules addressing transparency, manipulation, and high-risk automated systems. Several states are considering or implementing disclosure requirements and consumer protections involving AI-generated interactions and decision-making.

The direction is obvious. Regulators increasingly believe AI systems can influence human behavior in ways that deserve oversight.

Lawyers should not assume the profession will somehow remain exempt from that larger conversation.

Nor should lawyers make the mistake of thinking this issue concerns only litigation sanctions or fake citations. The larger danger may be much quieter.

Imagine a lawyer relying on AI to summarize thousands of pages of medical records and missing a critical notation buried in a chart. Imagine a firm using AI-generated intake systems that unintentionally discourage certain clients from pursuing valid claims. Imagine confidential settlement discussions being entered into unsecured platforms. Imagine billing disputes arising because clients discover they were charged attorney rates for work largely generated by software.

Those situations are coming. Some are probably already here.

What the profession needs now is not panic. It is structure.

Every law firm using AI should have written policies governing confidentiality, verification, supervision, disclosure, billing practices, and document review. Lawyers should understand what tools they are using, what risks those tools create, and what safeguards exist to protect clients. Firms should train attorneys the same way they train them on trust accounting or data security.

Most importantly, lawyers must remember that ethical responsibility cannot be delegated to software.

Not to ChatGPT. Not to Claude. Not to any platform that promises efficiency.

At the end of the day, the lawyer’s name is still the one appearing on the filing.

And that means the lawyer remains responsible for the judgment behind it.

Michael J. Epstein, a Harvard Law School graduate, is a trial lawyer and managing partner of The Epstein Law Firm, P.A., a law firm based in New Jersey.

The post Lawyers Do Not Need An AI Ban. They Need An AI Duty Of Care. appeared first on Above the Law.

articles.title: When Pay Is Equal, Boutique Firms Have The Edge
articles.content:
编者按：欢迎阅读我们的每日专栏“每日语录”。

“薪资不再是考虑因素，这样我就能专注于职业发展的其他方面，以及我想要达到的目标。”

——艾米丽·克拉克，威金森·斯特克洛夫律师事务所的合伙人。她在联邦书记员任期结束后，在米尔班克工作了一年，随后加入该事务所。她在向路透社发表的评论中表示，威金森的薪资水平与她加入这家精品律师事务所的部分原因相同。当前，法律行业高层正掀起一场薪资大战，精品事务所目前领先于大型律师事务所，这可能会鼓励更多律师进行横向流动。

斯蒂夫·扎雷茨基是Above the Law的执行主编，自2011年起在此工作。她非常乐意收到您的来信，欢迎通过电子邮件与她交流任何建议、问题、评论或批评。您可以在Bluesky、X/Twitter和Threads上关注她，或在LinkedIn上与她联系。

本文首次发布于Above the Law。
articles.summary:
Ed. note: Welcome to our daily feature, Quote of the Day.

It took the salary away as a factor, so I could focus on ​the other aspects of my career and where I wanted to be.

— Emily Clarke, an associate at Wilkinson Stekloff who joined the firm after a federal clerkship and a year at Milbank, in comments given to Reuters, concerning the fact that Wilkinson’s salary scale was the same as what inspired her, in part, to join the boutique firm. As a salary war unfolds in the upper echelons of legal profession, boutique firms are currently taking the lead over Biglaw firms, which may encourage more attorneys to make lateral moves.

Staci Zaretsky is the managing editor of Above the Law, where she’s worked since 2011. She’d love to hear from you, so please feel free to email her with any tips, questions, comments, or critiques. You can follow her on Bluesky, X/Twitter, and Threads, or connect with her on LinkedIn.

The post When Pay Is Equal, Boutique Firms Have The Edge appeared first on Above the Law.
